title: What is a Database Management System?
body: A DBMS is software that manages databases and provides mechanisms to store and retrieve data efficiently. It handles data organization and ensures data integrity.
detailed_explanation: A Database Management System (DBMS) is sophisticated software that serves as an intermediary between users/applications and the physical data storage. Key responsibilities include: (1) Data Storage - efficiently organizing data on disk with minimal redundancy; (2) Data Retrieval - enabling fast queries through indexing and optimization; (3) Data Modification - handling INSERT UPDATE DELETE with consistency; (4) Concurrency Control - allowing multiple users to access data simultaneously; (5) Data Security - protecting data through authentication and access control; (6) Backup & Recovery - ensuring no data loss during failures. Examples include MySQL PostgreSQL Oracle MongoDB and SQL Server.

title: Why do we need a DBMS?
body: List three problems without DBMS: data redundancy, data inconsistency, difficulty in data access and security issues.
detailed_explanation: Without a DBMS, applications manage data directly through file systems, creating critical problems: (1) Data Redundancy - same data stored in multiple files causing storage waste and update anomalies; (2) Data Inconsistency - related data becomes inconsistent when updates occur incompletely; (3) Access Difficulties - searching and modifying specific data requires complex custom code in each application; (4) Concurrency Issues - multiple users accessing files simultaneously cause data corruption; (5) Security Problems - no centralized access control allowing unauthorized data access; (6) No Recovery - system failures cause permanent data loss. DBMS solves all these through centralized management, ACID transactions, and query optimization.

title: DBMS vs File System
body: File systems store unstructured data; DBMS provides structured data management with queries and concurrent access.
detailed_explanation: File System (manual management): Data stored as files/folders; no built-in querying; application responsible for organizing and accessing data; manual locking for concurrent access; no automatic backup/recovery. DBMS (automatic management): Data stored in organized structured format (tables relations); powerful query language (SQL); automatic concurrency control; built-in backup/recovery mechanisms; enforces data integrity; optimizes performance. Analogy: Filing cabinet (file system) vs Library (DBMS) - library has cataloging organization and indexing making it far easier to search and retrieve information.

title: Advantages of DBMS
body: Data independence, data security, concurrent access, recovery mechanisms, and query optimization.
detailed_explanation: Primary DBMS Advantages: (1) Data Independence - applications don't depend on data storage format; schema changes don't break code; (2) Data Security - user authentication role-based access control encryption; (3) Concurrent Access - multiple users/applications access simultaneously without conflicts; (4) Recovery Mechanisms - logs checkpoints and backups enable recovery from crashes; (5) Query Optimization - automatic plan selection based on statistics; (6) Data Integrity - constraints triggers ensure valid data; (7) Minimal Redundancy - normalization processes reduce duplicate data; (8) Efficient Storage - indexing compression techniques reduce disk space; (9) Backup & Restore - simplified disaster recovery procedures.

title: Data Models in DBMS
body: Hierarchical (tree structure) vs Network (graph) vs Relational (tables) vs NoSQL (document/key-value).
detailed_explanation: Four major data models represent database structures: (1) Hierarchical Model - data organized in tree with parent-child relationships; single parent per node; navigation restricted; examples: IMS from IBM; rarely used today; (2) Network Model - graph structure allowing multiple parents; more flexible than hierarchical; complex navigation logic; examples: CODASYL; (3) Relational Model - tabular format with rows and columns; queries use SQL; dominant model today; examples: PostgreSQL MySQL Oracle; (4) Object-Oriented Model - objects containing data and methods; supports inheritance polymorphism; slower retrieval; (5) Document Model (NoSQL) - stores JSON/BSON documents; flexible schema; horizontal scaling; examples: MongoDB CouchDB; (6) Key-Value Model - simple key to value mapping; ultra-fast access; minimal querying; examples: Redis Memcached.

title: Relational Model fundamentals
body: Relations as tables; attributes as columns; tuples as rows; keys for uniqueness and relationships.
detailed_explanation: The Relational Model Foundation: (1) Relation - a table with a heading and body; heading contains distinct attribute names; body contains tuples (rows); (2) Attributes - named columns with defined domain (integer string date); each column has unique name within relation; (3) Tuples - rows representing individual records; order irrelevant; each tuple unique within relation; (4) Keys - Primary Key uniquely identifies tuple; Candidate Key is minimal key; Foreign Key references primary key in another relation; Composite Key uses multiple columns; (5) Constraints - domain constraints (valid values) entity integrity (primary key not null) referential integrity (foreign key references existing primary key); (6) Relationships - tables relate through foreign keys creating links between entities; normalization removes redundancy while maintaining relationships.

title: Entity Relationship Model
body: ER diagrams represent entities (objects) and relationships between them; basis for database design.
detailed_explanation: Entity-Relationship (ER) Model is high-level data modeling methodology: (1) Entities - objects with independent existence like Student Course Department; represented as rectangles; (2) Attributes - properties of entities like StudentID Name Email; represented as ovals attached to entity; (3) Relationships - associations between entities like Enrolls (Student enrolls in Course); represented as diamonds; (4) Cardinality - specifies relationship count; one-to-one (1:1) one-to-many (1:N) many-to-many (M:N); notations include crow's foot notation and min-max notation; (5) Weak Entity - depends on parent entity for uniqueness; lacks primary key; (6) Specialization/Generalization - hierarchy of entities (is-a relationships). ER models help understand requirements and serve as blueprint for relational schema design before physical implementation.

title: Introduction to SQL
body: SQL is declarative language for CRUD operations: CREATE READ UPDATE DELETE on relational databases.
detailed_explanation: Structured Query Language (SQL) is industry-standard language for relational databases: (1) Declarative - describe what you want not how to get it; database optimizes execution; (2) CRUD Operations - CREATE INSERT adds new data; READ SELECT retrieves data; UPDATE modifies existing data; DELETE removes data; (3) Data Definition Language (DDL) - CREATE TABLE ALTER TABLE DROP TABLE define database structures; (4) Data Manipulation Language (DML) - SELECT INSERT UPDATE DELETE manipulate data; (5) Data Control Language (DCL) - GRANT REVOKE manage permissions; (6) Transaction Control - COMMIT ROLLBACK SAVEPOINT manage transactions; (7) Standards - ANSI SQL is standard; vendors add extensions (T-SQL for SQL Server; PL/SQL for Oracle); (8) Advantage - universal language works across databases; easy to learn; powerful for complex queries.

title: SQL SELECT statement
body: SELECT retrieves data from tables with WHERE conditions, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, and aggregate functions.
detailed_explanation: SELECT Statement Syntax: SELECT [DISTINCT] column_list FROM table_name [WHERE condition] [GROUP BY columns] [HAVING condition] [ORDER BY columns] [LIMIT n]; (1) SELECT - specifies columns to retrieve; * returns all columns; DISTINCT eliminates duplicates; (2) FROM - source table(s); (3) WHERE - filters rows with conditions (=, <>, <, >, <=, >=, BETWEEN IN LIKE); logical operators AND OR NOT combine conditions; (4) GROUP BY - groups rows by column values; used with aggregate functions COUNT SUM AVG MIN MAX; (5) HAVING - filters groups based on aggregate values (like WHERE for grouped data); (6) ORDER BY - sorts results ASC ascending DESC descending; multiple columns allowed; (7) LIMIT - restricts number of returned rows; useful for pagination; (8) NULL handling - IS NULL IS NOT NULL; aggregate functions ignore NULL. Example: SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000 GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 60000 ORDER BY AVG(salary) DESC; retrieves departments with high average salaries.

title: Joins in SQL
body: INNER JOIN (matching rows), LEFT JOIN (all left + matching), RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN connect multiple tables.
detailed_explanation: SQL Join Types combine rows from multiple tables: (1) INNER JOIN - returns only matching rows from both tables based on condition; most common join; non-matching rows excluded; (2) LEFT JOIN (LEFT OUTER JOIN) - returns all rows from left table plus matching rows from right table; right table unmatching columns show NULL; useful when left table is primary; (3) RIGHT JOIN (RIGHT OUTER JOIN) - returns all rows from right table plus matching rows from left table; left table unmatching columns show NULL; (4) FULL OUTER JOIN - returns all rows from both tables; unmatched columns show NULL; combines LEFT and RIGHT JOIN results; (5) CROSS JOIN - Cartesian product of two tables; each row left table paired with every row right table; large result set; rarely used; (6) Self Join - table joined with itself; useful for hierarchical data like employee-manager relationships; (7) Natural Join - joins on all columns with same name automatically; less explicit than explicit ON clause. Practice: SELECT e.name m.name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id; shows each employee with their manager's name.

title: Normalization basics
body: Normalization eliminates redundancy through 1NF 2NF 3NF; reduces data anomalies and dependency issues.
detailed_explanation: Normalization Process systematically improves database design eliminating anomalies: (1) Anomalies occur in poorly designed databases - insertion anomaly (can't insert without complete data); deletion anomaly (losing valid data when deleting); update anomaly (inconsistency with duplicate updates); (2) Normalization Forms - progressive levels of normalization from 1NF through BCNF with each level removing specific dependency types; (3) Benefits - minimal redundancy (less storage and consistency issues); faster queries (smaller tables); easier maintenance; flexibility in adding new information; (4) Trade-offs - more tables require more joins (slightly slower queries); more complex schema to design and understand; (5) When to de-normalize - read-heavy systems with complex queries; denormalization intentionally reintroduces redundancy for performance; (6) Goal - achieve balance between redundancy elimination and query performance; typically aim for 3NF as sweet spot.

title: First Normal Form (1NF)
body: All attributes contain atomic values; no repeating groups or arrays in single fields.
detailed_explanation: First Normal Form Rules: (1) Atomic Values - each cell contains single indivisible value; no multi-valued attributes; (2) No Repeating Groups - related data stored separately rather than comma-separated in one field; (3) Example Violation - StudentPhones table with phone1 phone2 phone3 columns instead of separate phone records; (4) Correction - create PhoneNumbers table with foreign key to Student; (5) Repeating Groups Problem - difficult to query (which phone column?); hard to modify (if 4 phones needed?); wastes space (NULL values); (6) Benefits - standardized structure; easier to query; flexible for varying data counts; (7) Verification - no arrays arrays lists sets or composite values in columns; each column contains single atomic value; all rows have same columns. 1NF is foundation - all other normal forms assume 1NF; relative to table structure not absolute definition.

title: Second Normal Form (2NF)
body: Candidate key determines all non-prime attributes; remove partial dependencies between attributes and key.
detailed_explanation: Second Normal Form Requirements: (1) Prerequisites - must be in 1NF first; (2) Partial Dependency - non-prime attribute depends on part of composite primary key not entire key; (3) Example Violation - StudentCourse(StudentID CourseID CourseTitle Professor) where CourseTitle depends only on CourseID (part of key) not StudentID; (4) Problem - update anomaly (change professor name in one row but not another); insertion anomaly (can't add course until student enrolls); deletion anomaly (deleting student loses course info); (5) Solution - decompose into: StudentCourse(StudentID CourseID) and Course(CourseID CourseTitle Professor); (6) Verification - check each non-prime attribute depends on entire candidate key not subset; if table has simple primary key usually satisfies 2NF already; (7) Process - identify partial dependencies; move dependent attributes to new tables with subset of original key; (8) Result - eliminates anomalies related to composite keys.

title: Third Normal Form (3NF)
body: Non-prime attributes depend only on candidate key; remove transitive dependencies among non-prime attributes.
detailed_explanation: Third Normal Form Requirements: (1) Prerequisites - must be in 2NF first; (2) Transitive Dependency - non-prime attribute A depends on non-prime attribute B which depends on key; indirect dependency through non-key column; (3) Example Violation - Student(StudentID Name DepartmentID DepartmentName Building) where DepartmentName and Building depend on DepartmentID which itself depends on key; (4) Problem - update anomaly (change department building in one record but not others); insertion anomaly (can't add department without student); deletion anomaly (losing department info); (5) Solution - decompose into: Student(StudentID Name DepartmentID) and Department(DepartmentID DepartmentName Building); (6) Verification - draw dependency diagram; identify chains (key -> attribute -> attribute); break chains by creating new tables; (7) Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) - every determinant must be candidate key; stricter than 3NF handles rare cases where 3NF fails; (8) Practical Target - 3NF achieves most redundancy reduction; BCNF rarely necessary; benefits usually outweigh complexity.

title: ACID properties
body: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions), Durability (persistent).
detailed_explanation: ACID Properties Ensure Database Reliability: (1) Atomicity - all-or-nothing execution; transaction either completes fully or rolls back completely; if system crashes mid-transaction everything reverts; prevents partial updates corrupting data; (2) Consistency - database moves from one valid state to another valid state; constraints constraints business rules data types enforced; invalid data never committed; (3) Isolation - concurrent transactions don't interfere; one transaction can't see uncommitted changes from another; prevents dirty reads; lost updates; phantom reads depending on isolation level; (4) Durability - once committed data persists; survives system failures; write to permanent storage before confirming commit; uses write-ahead logging and backups. Example: Bank transfer from Account A to Account B: Atomicity ensures both debit and credit complete or neither occurs; Consistency ensures account balances stay accurate and non-negative; Isolation ensures concurrent transfers don't corrupt balances; Durability ensures committed transfer survives power loss. ACID violations cause data corruption financial loss inconsistency; critical for any mission-critical system.

title: Transactions and ACID
body: Explain how banks ensure ACID properties during fund transfers between accounts.
detailed_explanation: Bank Transfer ACID Implementation: Scenario: Transfer 1000 from Account A to Account B. (1) Atomicity - system creates single transaction encapsulating: BEGIN TRANSACTION; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 1000 WHERE id = A; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 1000 WHERE id = B; COMMIT; If crash between updates transaction rolls back both. (2) Consistency - constraints prevent negative balance or missing amounts; trigger ensures A decreases and B increases by same amount; (3) Isolation - if concurrent transfers occur isolation level prevents dirty reads; READ COMMITTED level allows other transactions see only committed balances; (4) Durability - after COMMIT returns the 1000 transfer is permanent in database backup logs even if server explodes; (5) Safety Mechanisms - write-ahead logging records transaction intent before execution allowing recovery; checkpoints periodically flush committed data to disk; transaction logs maintain complete history for recovery; (6) Problems Without ACID - Atomicity failure: A debited but B not credited (money lost); Consistency failure: negative balance; Isolation failure: two transactions see conflicting balances; Durability failure: system crash loses committed transactions.

title: Concurrency Control basics
body: Multiple users accessing database simultaneously; prevent conflicts like lost updates and dirty reads.
detailed_explanation: Concurrency Control manages simultaneous database access: (1) Concurrency Problems - Lost Update (T1 and T2 both read then write causing T1 overwritten); Dirty Read (T1 reads uncommitted change from T2 which then rolls back); Non-Repeatable Read (T1 reads twice gets different values T2 modified between reads); Phantom Read (T1 queries twice gets different row count due T2 insert/delete); (2) Need for Control - improve throughput and response time by allowing overlapping transactions; prevent data corruption from simultaneous access; (3) Serializability - transaction execution should be equivalent to some serial order (one at time); ensures correctness; (4) Isolation Levels - READ UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads allowed fastest); READ COMMITTED (dirty reads prevented); REPEATABLE READ (phantom reads possible); SERIALIZABLE (strictest guarantees no anomalies); (5) Trade-off - strictest isolation slowest performance; loosest isolation fastest performance; database administrators tune for balance; (6) Techniques - locks pessimistic assumes conflicts likely; timestamps optimistic assumes conflicts rare; MVCC multi-version provides consistent snapshots.

title: Lock-based protocols
body: Shared locks for reads; exclusive locks for writes. Deadlock occurs when processes wait circularly.
detailed_explanation: Lock-Based Concurrency Control: (1) Shared Lock (Read Lock) - multiple transactions can hold simultaneously on same resource; prevents writes but allows reads; (2) Exclusive Lock (Write Lock) - only one transaction can hold; prevents all other access (read or write); (3) Lock Protocol - Two-Phase Locking (2PL) prevents conflicts: growing phase acquires locks; shrinking phase releases locks; prevents most anomalies; (4) Deadlock - occurs when two transactions wait for each other's locks in circle: T1 locks A waits for B; T2 locks B waits for A; requires external intervention; (5) Deadlock Detection - WAIT-FOR graph tracks who waits for whom; cycle detection identifies deadlock; victim transaction rolled back releasing locks; (6) Deadlock Prevention - resource ordering (always acquire in same order); timeout (release all locks after timeout); or conservative 2PL (acquire all locks before starting); (7) Starvation - transaction never acquires all needed locks waiting forever; queue management and fairness policies prevent; (8) Granularity - lock page row table hierarchy; finer granularity more concurrent but more overhead; (9) Databases use variations like row-level locking page-level locking optimizations to balance concurrency and overhead.

title: Indexing and Performance
body: Indexes speed up data retrieval like book index; types include primary index clustered index and secondary index.
detailed_explanation: Database Indexing Fundamentals: (1) Index Purpose - speeds up SELECT queries WHERE clauses; slower than full table scan minimal overhead; trade-off storage space for query speed; (2) Primary Index - built on primary key; guarantees uniqueness; often clustered (determines physical row order); (3) Clustered Index - determines physical order rows stored on disk; only one per table; non-clustered index points to data location via clustered key; (4) Secondary Index (Non-Clustered) - multiple allowed per table; index on columns besides primary key; requires lookup to find actual rows; (5) Index Storage - separate structure (B-tree hash table) maintained by DBMS; updates on insert update delete require index maintenance; (6) When to Index - high cardinality columns (many unique values); frequently searched columns; reduce full table scans; (7) When NOT to Index - low cardinality (few unique values); small tables; columns rarely searched; write-heavy tables (index maintenance overhead); (8) Query Planning - optimizer chooses index if cost lower than scan; statistics determine row count estimates; good indexes crucial for performance; (9) Maintenance - periodic index rebuild reorganization; monitor index fragmentation; remove unused indexes consuming space.

title: Hash Indexing
body: Hash function maps key to bucket location; O(1) search but poor for range queries.
detailed_explanation: Hash Index Structure and Performance: (1) Hash Function - maps key value to bucket location uniformly distributing entries; perfect hash function unique value produces unique location; (2) Bucket - storage unit in hash index containing multiple key-value pairs; collision when multiple keys hash to same bucket; (3) Collision Handling - chaining (linked list in bucket) or open addressing (probe other buckets); affects lookup performance; (4) Lookup Operation - compute hash(key) locate bucket retrieve value; O(1) average case extremely fast equality searches; (5) Advantages - constant time search regardless of table size; simple implementation; good for equality conditions; (6) Disadvantages - no range query support (can't find keys between 100-200); no sorting; keys not ordered; rebuilt entire index if rehashing needed when too full; (7) Use Cases - lookup tables small sets equality selections primary key access; (8) Range Inefficiency - hash doesn't preserve order; range queries require scanning entire index finding all matching buckets; B-tree preferred when range queries common; (9) Database Usage - some systems like MySQL Memory engine support hash indexes; most use B-tree by default; trade-offs between access patterns determine choice.

title: B-Tree and B+ Tree Index
body: Balanced tree structures maintain sorted order. B+ Tree uses leaf pointers for range queries; used in most databases.
detailed_explanation: B-Tree and B+ Tree Indexing: (1) B-Tree Properties - self-balancing tree structure; maintains sorted order logarithmic height log_d(n) where d is branching factor; minimize disk I/O; (2) Nodes - internal nodes contain keys and pointers; leaf nodes contain key-value pairs or pointers to data; all leaves at same depth; (3) B-Tree Search - start root compare key decide branch continue recursively; logarithmic time complexity; supports both equality and range searches; (4) B+ Tree Improvements - all keys in leaf nodes only; internal nodes contain index keys for navigation; leaf nodes linked together (linked list); (5) Advantages of B+ Tree - leaf linking enables efficient range query scanning; simpler insertion deletion only affects leaves; internal nodes never modified; (6) Example - B+ Tree with order 3: internal nodes have 2-3 children; leaves hold actual data; range query 'find keys 50-100' enters at root follows branches to leaf node then scans linked leaf nodes; (7) Database Usage - PostgreSQL MySQL InnoDB default to B+ Tree indexes; proven efficient for disk-based storage; millions of rows still quick access; (8) Performance - insert update delete operations O(log n); full table scan O(n) much slower; creating index trades storage for query speed; most important for large tables frequently queried.

title: Query Optimization
body: Optimizer estimates cost of query plans and chooses efficient execution path; uses indexes and statistics.
detailed_explanation: Query Optimization Process: (1) Query Parsing - parser ensures syntactic correctness converts SQL to internal representation; semantic checking validates table column references; (2) Optimization Stages - logical optimization (remove redundant calculations) independent of physical storage; physical optimization (choose access methods indexes) considers available indexes table statistics; (3) Plan Generation - consider multiple execution plans: full scan vs index scan; join ordering (different order for joins); different index combinations; (4) Cost Estimation - cardinality estimation predicts row counts at each step using statistics (column value distribution distinct values); I/O cost (disk reads) dominates CPU cost; (5) Statistics Importance - outdated statistics cause poor plans selecting slow execution; ANALYZE UPDATE STATS necessary after bulk inserts deletes; histogram distribution information helps; (6) Join Strategy Selection - nested loop join simple slower scans inner for each outer row; sort merge join fast if both sorted hash join fast for large joins; (7) Index Usage - optimizer chooses index if cost lower than table scan; selectivity (percentage matching rows) determines benefit; (8) Execution Plan Visualization - EXPLAIN EXPLAIN ANALYZE show chosen plan execution statistics; professionals use plans identify bottlenecks; (9) Hints - query hints override optimizer decisions; sometimes manual hints faster; USE INDEX FORCE INDEX in MySQL.

title: Backup and Recovery
body: Backup copies of database; Recovery restores after failure using logs and checkpoints; WAL (Write-Ahead Logging).
detailed_explanation: Backup and Recovery Strategies: (1) Backup Types - Full Backup (entire database) expensive but simple; Incremental Backup (changes since last backup) fast but complex restore; Differential Backup (changes since full backup) balance; (2) Backup Frequency - trade-off RPO (Recovery Point Objective) how much data loss tolerable vs backup time network load; (3) Backup Location - local disk fast restore; offsite cloud geographically separate survives disasters; (4) Recovery Process - restore from backup replay from logs; restore latest backup from disk then replay transaction log to recover committed changes post-backup before crash; (5) Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) - log changes before writing to database file; if crash log survives data loss prevented; most databases use WAL principle; (6) Checkpoints - periodic snapshots of database state; recovery only needs logs after latest checkpoint not entire log; accelerates recovery; (7) Recovery Types - crash recovery (restart database replays log); restoration recovery (restore older backup due corruption); (8) Testing - regularly test backups verify restore works; many organizations discover backup failure only during disaster unacceptable delay; (9) Tools - MySQL backup mysqldump xtrabackup; PostgreSQL backup pg_dump pg_basebackup; commercial tools often included.

title: Data Replication
body: Copies database to multiple servers for availability and fault tolerance; master-slave or peer-to-peer replication.
detailed_explanation: Database Replication Methods: (1) Master-Slave Replication - one master accepts writes replicates to slave(s); slaves read-only; advantages master down slaves continue reads; disadvantages single write bottleneck; (2) Master-Master Replication - multiple masters accept writes; highly available but complex conflict resolution; used rarely; (3) Asynchronous Replication - master returns success immediately; slave replication delayed; fast write response; potential data loss if master fails; (4) Synchronous Replication - master waits slave confirmation; ensures consistency safety; slower higher latency; (5) Statement-Based Replication - master sends SQL commands to slave; space-efficient but non-deterministic statements cause divergence; (6) Row-Based Replication - master sends actual row changes; deterministic accurate but higher volume; modern approach; (7) Hybrid (Mixed-Mode) - use statement replication when possible row replication for non-deterministic; (8) Failover - detect master failure switch clients to slave promote slave to master; automated tools like MySQL Router; (9) Uses - read scaling distribute query load across slaves; backup (replication to backup server); analytics (separate slave for reporting); disaster recovery (geographically distributed replicas); (10) Challenges - replication lag (time delay between master write and slave visibility); network partitions split-brain; consistency guarantees depending on sync replication.

title: DBMS Architecture layers
body: External (views), Conceptual (logical schema), Internal (physical storage) three-level architecture.
detailed_explanation: Three-Level DBMS Architecture Abstraction: (1) External Level (Views) - multiple user views of database; customized for different users/applications; hides irrelevant details; examples: HR department sees employee names salaries; payroll sees different fields; (2) Conceptual Level (Logical Schema) - unified logical database structure; entity relationships constraints; independent of physical storage; what developers see designing schemas; (3) Internal Level (Physical Schema) - how data actually stored on disk; file organizations indexes structures; machine-specific; (4) Data Independence - logical data independence: application unaffected by conceptual schema changes (adding optional column); physical data independence: application unaffected by storage changes (different hardware file structures); (5) Mapping - DBMS maintains mappings between levels; external to conceptual mapping (view definitions); conceptual to internal mapping (storage structures); (6) Benefits - users only interact external level relevant to them; developers use conceptual schema; administrators manage internal optimization; (7) Example - Conceptual: Employee(ID Name Department Salary); External View1: Employee(ID Name Department) for marketing no salary; External View2: Employee(Salary) for HR only; Internal: hash index on ID B-tree on Department; (8) Abstraction Advantages - change storage format without affecting external views; add new fields without modifying applications using other fields; different views provide security hiding sensitive data.

title: Storage Management
body: Buffer management, disk I/O optimization, record organization (heap sequential indexed sequential) and access.
detailed_explanation: Storage Management Components: (1) Buffer Management - cache frequently accessed pages in memory; reduce disk I/O; buffer pool holds multiple pages; (2) Replacement Policies - LRU (Least Recently Used) evict oldest accessed; LFU (Least Frequently Used) evict least used; CLOCK approximate LRU efficient; (3) Physical Organization - heap file (unordered pages) insertion anywhere but scans slow; sequential file (ordered by key) scans fast but insertion expensive; indexed sequential (sequential with index) balanced access; (4) Records Storage - fixed-length records easy offset calculation; variable-length records flexible space efficient but harder locate; (5) Page Structure - pages fixed size (4KB 8KB) basic storage unit; record directory within page tracks records offsets; deletions marked with tombstones; (6) Record Addressing - absolute address page number offset within page; physical addressing (disk block) vs logical addressing (translates to physical); (7) I/O Optimization - prefetch (read ahead when sequential pattern detected); caching (keep hot pages in memory); batching (group multiple I/O operations); (8) Disk Access Time - seek time dominates; minimize seeks by clustering related data; sequential access faster than random; (9) Fragmentation - external fragmentation (unused spaces between records); internal fragmentation (wasted space within record); periodic reorganization recovers space; (10) Trade-offs - faster access requires more storage overhead; careful balance required.

title: NoSQL Databases
body: Non-relational databases: document (MongoDB) key-value (Redis) graph (Neo4j) column-family (Cassandra).
detailed_explanation: NoSQL Database Categories: (1) Document Databases - store JSON BSON documents; flexible schema each document differs; examples MongoDB CouchDB Firebase; use case web applications content management; (2) Key-Value Stores - simple mapping key to value; extremely fast in-memory access; examples Redis Memcached DynamoDB; use case caching sessions leaderboards; (3) Graph Databases - nodes and edges represent relationships; optimized for highly connected data; examples Neo4j ArangoDB; use case social networks recommendation systems knowledge graphs; (4) Column-Family Stores - organize data by column; efficient compression columnar analytics; examples Cassandra HBase Bigtable; use case time-series wide tables; (5) Search Engines - full-text search analytics; examples Elasticsearch Solr; use case logs analysis search; (6) Advantages over Relational - horizontal scalability (add servers); flexible schema (evolve without downtime); performance (optimized for specific access patterns); (7) Trade-offs - eventual consistency (not ACID); no complex joins; schema design still important; learning curve; (8) When to Use - massive data volumes (petabytes); high throughput (millions operations); flexible structure (not fixed schema); (9) When NOT to Use - complex transactions (banking); joins across multiple entities; structured data (well-defined schema); (10) Polyglot Persistence - use multiple databases; PostgreSQL for structured; MongoDB for flexible; Redis for caching; Neo4j for relationships; choose right tool for job.

title: MongoDB basics
body: Document-oriented database; flexible schema; JSON-like documents; horizontal scalability with sharding.
detailed_explanation: MongoDB Fundamentals: (1) Document Model - stores data as BSON documents (JSON binary format); flexible schema each document structure can differ; no predefined schema; (2) Collections - analogous to tables; contains multiple documents; no schema enforced; (3) Queries - query language similar to SQL but JSON-style; db.collection.find({name: 'John'}) retrieves documents; (4) Indexing - supports indexes on any field; B-tree indexes improve query performance; (_id field automatically indexed); (5) CRUD Operations - Create: insertOne insertMany; Read: find findOne; Update: updateOne updateMany replace; Delete: deleteOne deleteMany; (6) Aggregation - pipeline operations process documents: match (filter) project (select fields) group (aggregate) sort limit; (7) Sharding - horizontal scaling across servers; data distributed by shard key; each shard responsible for subset; (8) Replication - replica sets provide redundancy; primary accepts writes; secondaries maintain copies; automatic failover if primary fails; (9) Advantages - schema flexibility (evolve quickly); nested documents (reduce joins); scalability (sharding); developer-friendly (JSON familiar); (10) Disadvantages - no multi-document transactions (until version 4.0); larger document size vs relational normalized; memory usage higher; (11) Use Cases - content management systems; IoT sensor data; mobile app backends; real-time analytics; (12) vs Relational - trade ACID transactions for scalability; trade normalization for flexibility.

title: Distributed Databases
body: Data spread across multiple locations; challenges: consistency eventual consistency CAP theorem.
detailed_explanation: Distributed Database Challenges: (1) Motivation - scalability (exceed single server capacity); availability (survive node failures); performance (reduce latency client-local data); disaster recovery; (2) Distribution Strategies - horizontal partitioning (different rows different servers); vertical partitioning (different columns different servers); replication (full copy multiple servers); (3) Consistency Models - strong consistency (all see latest immediately) slow expensive; eventual consistency (replicas converge over time) fast but temporary inconsistency possible; (4) Network Partitions - communication failures split system into isolated components; cannot both maintain consistency and availability during partition; (5) CAP Theorem Simplified - Cannot simultaneously guarantee all three: Consistency (all see same data); Availability (all nodes operational); Partition tolerance (survive network splits); (6) System Trade-offs - CA (traditional relational some fail without partition recovery); CP (strong consistency accept unavailability); AP (eventual consistency remain available). Banking (chooses consistency); social media (chooses availability); (7) Split-Brain Problem - two components claim leadership creating conflicts; requires consensus protocols; (8) Timestamp Ordering - globally synchronize clocks (hard in distributed); use logical clocks (Lamport clocks vector clocks); (9) Consensus Protocols - Paxos RAFT achieve agreement despite failures; used in distributed databases; complex but necessary; (10) Examples - Google Spanner global transactions across data centers; Cassandra eventual consistency; Bigtable immutable architecture.

title: CAP Theorem
body: Cannot guarantee all three: Consistency Availability Partition tolerance. Trade-offs in distributed systems.
detailed_explanation: CAP Theorem Explained: (1) Consistency - every read returns latest written data; all clients see same version; ACID-like guarantees; (2) Availability - every request receives response; no timeouts or errors; system always operational; (3) Partition Tolerance - system continues despite network failures; nodes isolated from each other; communication delays or losses; (4) Theorem Statement - in distributed system with network partitions impossible implement all three properties; must sacrifice one; (5) Why Impossible - partition occurs some nodes unreachable; to provide consistency: all nodes must agree (requires communication across partition - impossible); to provide availability during partition: nodes respond locally immediately (data becomes inconsistent); cannot have both; (6) Real World - networks unreliable partitions happen (Murphy's law); partition tolerance not optional; choice between consistency and availability; (7) Consistency-Favoring (CP) - block writes during partition ensure strong consistency; examples: traditional databases with locking; some financial systems; disadvantage: unavailable during partition; (8) Availability-Favoring (AP) - accept writes during partition merge later; eventual consistency; examples: social networks caching layers; disadvantage: temporary inconsistency reads may return old data; (9) Practical Considerations - false dichotomy sometimes: using replication quorums can mitigate; CRDT Conflict-free Replicated Data Types auto-merge safely; careful design can achieve both; (10) Lessons - understand trade-offs when designing distributed systems; no silver bullet; choose based on requirements (finance needs C; social media OK with eventual).
